experiments.title: How a testimonial email from a donor performed against a faux-forward email during a fundraising appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donor testimonial email from a real donor for people receiving a campaign appeal email will achieve an increase in email clicks to the donation page because personal stories can increase social proof and encourage readers to join them by becoming a donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
During the critical calendar year-end fundraising period, Luther Seminary wanted to test the effectiveness of their Giving Tuesday PM email communication strategy. The traditional approach has used a faux-forward email, where the PM message includes a “forwarded” copy of the AM email, with a brief added note from the person forwarding it.
For this experiment, we tested the faux-forward strategy against a Treatment email that replaced the forwarded content with a testimonial from a real donor, Greg, sharing his personal reasons for supporting Luther Seminary. This test aimed to determine whether focusing on a donor testimonial would lead to higher engagement and more conversions compared to the repetitive messaging of the faux-forward email.
Donor stories would create stronger emotional connections and trust, motivating readers to act and contribute to Luther Seminary’s mission. A personal endorsement from a peer would resonate more deeply with recipients, leading to increased clicks to the donation page. At the same time, the faux-forward approach might contribute to disengagement or message fatigue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we found that the Treatment email, featuring a testimonial from a donor (Greg), led to a -39% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with a 97.9% statistical level of confidence. This result was unexpected, as we hypothesized that the personal donor story would increase trust and emotional connection, ultimately driving more clicks to the donation page.

One major factor contributing to this decrease could be the -21.8% decrease in email opens for the Treatment email, with a 100% statistical level of confidence. This drop in opens suggests that the unfamiliar sender, Greg Myhr, may have impacted the email's ability to capture the recipient’s attention. People are more likely to open emails from familiar senders, a concept known as sender recognition or trust transfer. In this case, emails from Luther Seminary staff may have felt more familiar and trustworthy, prompting more recipients to open the email.

Psychologically, cognitive fluency plays a role here: people are more likely to engage with content that feels easy, familiar, and comfortable. When the email is from a known and trusted source (like Luther Seminary staff), recipients may feel more at ease engaging with the content. When the email comes from an unfamiliar source (like a donor), it may subconsciously signal uncertainty or a potential risk, causing people to hesitate or overlook the email altogether.

Additionally, while social proof can be a powerful motivator, it needs to be framed in a way that maximizes its impact. Although Greg’s testimonial aimed to leverage the principle of social proof by showing how a fellow donor supports Luther Seminary, the email’s unfamiliar sender might have diminished its credibility. In-group bias suggests that people tend to trust and engage more with others who belong to their perceived social group or network. Because Greg was an outsider to the recipients (compared to the staff sender), the emotional pull of his testimonial may have been weakened.

Another contributing factor could be cognitive overload. The Treatment email included a personal story from Greg, which while emotionally powerful, might have added a layer of complexity or unfamiliarity that decreased its effectiveness compared to the more straightforward faux-forward email (the Control). The faux-forward email, which came from a familiar source, was simpler and less cognitively demanding for the reader, making it easier to process and act upon.

This suggests that while donor testimonials are effective in fostering connection and trust, the familiarity of the sender is crucial in determining how much attention and engagement the email receives. If the recipient doesn't recognize or trust the sender immediately, they may be less likely to open the email and, as a result, less likely to engage with the content, including the donation link.

experiments.title: Will longer copy help donors convert on a lower third slide up?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more copy value proposition for the lower third slide up will achieve more transactions because the additional value prop will provide social proof and clarity into what their gift is doing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2025
Focus on the Family is a global ministry that is a well recognized brand. They drive tons of traffic to their site daily.
Right now, they are in the middle of a sustainer drive. To support this campaign we added what we call a ‘lower third’ slideup element to their content pages. We wanted to see if long or short copy worked best on the these pages to convert donors. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Long copy, which includes a value proposition for the lower third slideup, was more effective in converting donors compared to short copy.

2. The additional value proposition provided social proof and clarity, which likely contributed to the higher donation rate.

3. The treatment resulted in a significant increase in donations, with a 98.3% level of confidence that the results were not due to random chance.

In future experimentation, we should:

1. Continue testing different copy lengths and value propositions to further optimize donation conversions.

2. Consider incorporating social proof and clear explanations of how donations will be used in our messaging.

3. Regularly analyze and track the performance of different elements to identify what resonates best with our audience and drives the most meaningful actions.

experiments.title: How a Testimonial Carousel Compares to a Slide-Up with Gift Array
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a testimony carousel for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because in comparison to the normal slideup, it helps incorporate and visualize the real impact a donor’s gift has on those who use the CaringBridge platform every day.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2024 - 12/14/2024
During CaringBridge’s high-urgency appeal, we hypothesized that a testimony carousel would prove more emotive and effective at converting donors than the standard slide-up. In the control, users were presented with a slide-up that contained three giving options. In the treatment, users were presented with a similar slide-up onsite, but besides the copy was a rotating carousel of real images and stories from the CaringBridge blog about who is impacted by the platform.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the testimony carousel, which was the treatment, did not achieve an increase in donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, it led to a significant decrease of 62.3% in Donations compared to the control.
This outcome suggests that the rotating carousel of real images and stories from the CaringBridge blog may have been less effective or possibly distracting for users, leading to a lower conversion rate. This may also indicate that the effectiveness of the control, is in using that right-hand side of the slideup in order to simplify the giving process rather than expanding on more reasons to give. Having a gift array that prefills on the donation page, is ultimately more impactful on the donor's completion of their gift than the additional testimonies on the carousel.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of conducting thorough testing and being open to unexpected outcomes in order to make informed decisions for future strategies and optimizations.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to enhance authority impacted course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal, clarity, and authority through a testimonial for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 11/18/2022
In a previous experiment, we had seen that adding a personal quote from the professor on the course registration page increased sign-ups. We wanted to test this same hypothesis on additional courses to see if this is something that was specific to the previous course or specific quote or if this was a global concept that could be rolled out on all courses taught by the seminary’s president. For this course, we added a brief testimonial and a photo of the professor so potential course registrants can get a glimpse of additional clarity about who is teaching the course and how having social proof from a figure of authority would motivate visitors to give away their email address on the page. We split the traffic 50/50 to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we discovered after running the experiment for one month was that the treatment lifted the conversion rate on the acquisition page by 7.4% with 97.2% confidence. Drilling down into specific segments, we did notice a directional decrease in conversion from paid social, which will need to be addressed. However, on its face we believe this treatment layout makes the course more appealing by humanizing the offer. Since we have now seen similar treatments provide lifts on various courses regardless of the course-specific testimonial content, we will add this feature to the existing layout of the course registration pages for those that are taught by the seminary president.

experiments.title: How a testimonial on a landing page impacted sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a testimonial for landing page visitors will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is validity in the value of the offer. because  there is validity in the value of the offer. because  there is validity in the value of the offer. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/02/2023 - 11/30/2023
WUNC is a local NPR station in North Carolina.
“Things To Do in North Carolina” is a content offer provided by WUNC in an effort to acquire new names and donors.
In an effort to increase conversions, we decided to add a testimony that WUNC received from someone who has requested the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a testimonial to the landing page for the "Things To Do in North Carolina" offer resulted in a higher sign up rate. The treatment group that saw the landing page with the testimonial had a 14.7% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group.
This suggests that including a testimonial adds validity and trust to the offer, making it more appealing to potential donors. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of testimonials or social proof elements on landing pages for different content offers or donation campaigns. Additionally, it would be worth exploring other methods to build trust and credibility, such as featuring testimonials from well-known individuals or partnering with influencers in the community.
Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of social proof in increasing conversions and can guide future optimization strategies for acquiring new names and donors for the nonprofit.

experiments.title: How social proof and communal language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update(Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using social proof and communal language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will identify with being a compassionate person and will want to join others who have donated.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using social proof/communal language to let donors know that they’re joining a larger group of compassionate people when they give. The last test we ran for this experiment generated a decrease in gifts. We had tested using fear of loss language so our hypothesis is that CaringBridge’s audience may respond more positively to joining in on something rather than missing out on something.
The first time we ran this experiment CaringBridge was undergoing a change in donation platform. This additional variable introduced during the experiment did not give us the clean data needed. We rested this concept to gather better data.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using social proof and communal language in JEN emails led to a significant decrease in donations for all traffic. This experiment resulted in a 34% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99% - making this a valid result. This confirms that the initial experiment is also a valid result in the decrease in donor conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to explore other messaging strategies that may resonate better with the audience and use other psychological principles to move prospective donors to action.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a petition page affects email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an element of social proof for potential petition signers will achieve a higher email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2022 - 11/12/2022
MSLF is running a petition as an acquisition offer. We wanted to test how adding social proof to the petition page of how many people have already signed the petition would affect email conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The social proof element caused a 6.8% increase in email acquisition with a 99.8% level of confidence. We believe this means that users are more likely to sign a petition when they see the evidence of others signing and believing in the value proposition as well.

Although we saw a valid increase in email acquisition, we saw a directional decrease of 28.6% for revenue received on the following instant donation page. We would like to test this concept further on other acquisition offers to see if we have the same results for revenue.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 28.6% decrease with a 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increased appeal through value proposition and social proof impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increased appeal through value proposition and social proof for potential coursetakers will achieve an increase in course sign ups because potential coursetakers will be inspired by what the course offers them and by the overall popularity of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/08/2023
In an effort to increase course sign ups through their Revelations course registration page, DTS has developed a treatment to communicate enhanced appeal for the offer and leverage social proof.
For the treatment, we’re refocusing the value proposition on the potential course taker rather than the organization and the course professor. We want to test the angle of “what can this course do for me” or “what can I get out of this course”. And we also want to leverage social proof to see if joining “one of our most popular courses” or “30,000 people who have already enrolled” will motivate people to take the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our combination of increased appeal and social proof produced a valid 62.7% decrease in course sign ups during a time span of only 13 days.
It seems this approach was not well-received by our audience, as we saw a decrease in all segments and down-funnel at the donations and revenue levels as well.
Perhaps our audience does appreciate the background of the professor of the course and learning about the organization's purpose for their courses as they get ready to sign up – more so than learning what they may get out of this specific course. Additionally, social proof may not move the needle if someone is interested in the course from a personal education standpoint rather than a "popularity" stand point.
Ultimately, we may want to test different value proposition that better compliments the course instructor and organization's mission instead of taking an entirely different angle like we did in this experiment. We might also test different types of social proof such as testimonials instead of popularity call outs.
We can isolate these elements to get better direction for future tests.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials to an education program sign up page impacted sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including parent testimonials of the program on the main registration page will result in an increase in email acquisition because the social proof of other’s reviews will encourage more people to sign up for the program.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2023 - 04/14/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to test how adding testimonials from parents about the value of the Little Patriots program would affect the email acquisition rate for ACI.
We split traffic 50/50 – 50% received the control registration page while the other 50% received the treatment version which included the testimonials/reviews.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including parent testimonials on the registration page does not necessarily lead to an increase in email acquisition rates. In fact, the treatment version with testimonials saw a 10.4% decrease in email acquisitions for all traffic, with a 96.1% level of confidence that this result was statistically significant. This suggests that the testimonials may have been distracting to the overall goal of email acquisition, and added unnecessary friction to the registration process.
The treatment saw a 9.2% decrease in email acquisition among new visitors with a 90% level of confidence. This is encouraging as even new visitors to Little Patriots come to the registration page already confident in the product. They do not need testimonials to convert.
Based on these findings, future experimentation should focus on testing the impact of testimonials on different types of visitors, as well as exploring alternative forms of social proof, such as trust badges or customer ratings. Additionally, it may be useful to test the impact of different types of language and messaging on the registration page to identify the most effective call-to-action for each type of visitor.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial from an authority figure for free online course visitors will achieve an increase in email registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 11/18/2022
On a previous test, we found that including a testimonial from an authority figure on a course registration page improved email conversion. We were curious if this optimization could be expanded to include additional courses so we developed a treatment that highlighted a quote and photo of the free online course professor to enhance the appeal, credibility, and clarity offered on the course registration page.
experiments.keyLearnings: Surprisingly this is the first time we observed a decrease in email acquisition by including a photo and quote on the course registration page. After running the experiment, we saw a 13.9% decrease in email conversion with 99% level of confidence. Multiple visitor segments experienced declined including email visitors, returning visitors, mobile visitors, and paid social visitors. It may be worthwhile to examine this quote compared to other quotes for alternate courses where a similar experiment was also run to see if there are any obvious differences that could be influencing the results. For a next iteration, we could choose a testimonial quote to feature that focuses more on what the user will receive or learn through the course rather than simply giving additional information about the book the course is centered around.

experiments.title: How removing a progress thermometer to a donation page affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a progress thermometer on a campaign donation page will result in lower conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 08/08/2022
In previous high-urgency campaigns, we’ve had success increasing conversion rates by showing a progress thermometer on the top of the donation page once we were nearing our goal. Knowing this, we wanted to see if we could replicate these results during a recent high-urgency campaign for a public policy client. We hoped to increase conversion rates by inspiring prospects to be one of the donors that helped “fill the bar” and help us meet our goal.
This concept attempts to employ the principles of urgency (we only have X to go), social proof (many others found this cause worthy to support), and unity (belonging to a group) in an effort to inspire donors to act.
We initially planned to show the progress thermometer to 100% of traffic, but then we decided to test this concept again and instead split traffic 50/50 between the control (including the progress thermometer) and the treatment (without the progress thermometer), hypothesizing that removing the thermometer would reduce conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite previous success using the progress thermometer concept during high urgency campaigns, in this instance removing the thermometer produced a valid lift of 17.1% at a 99% level of confidence.
Interestingly, while removing the progress thermometer increased donations, it also resulted in an 8.7% decrease in revenue. And while we were unable to validate this decrease (75.9% level of confidence), when we combine this finding with the fact that the average gift given by donors increased by 24.8% with the thermometer, it appears that while removing the thermometer increased the amount of gifts received, including the thermometer improved the quality of gifts and ultimately raised more money—presumably by motivating donors to give more so that the goal could be reached and because the amount of money already raised indicated that this was a worthy cause to support.
Because of the mixed results produced by this experiment, it may be worth seeking additional opportunities to test this hypothesis again, focusing on revenue, in an effort to validate the portion of our findings that indicated that including the progress thermometer increased overall revenue during the time this experiment was run.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and credibility from a relevant leadership figure impacted giving on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a relevant leadership figure for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 05/14/2022
The main donation page for DTS had for a long time featured a quote and photo of their Chancellor and well-known pastor, Chuck Swindoll— included to enhance the Seminary’s credibility in training pastors as people are deciding to make a gift. With a transition in DTS leadership, they had a new Chancellor, Dr. Mark Bailey. Rather than updating the page with a quote and image of Dr. Bailey, we wanted to test into it to see what impact, if any, it had on donations. We also hypothesized whether we should feature the DTS Chancellor or the DTS President, as the President has more current visibility and relevance. We were also curious if featuring someone who was a professor for multiple free online courses would be more recognizable and familiar to page visitors. We left the Chancellor Emeritus quote and photo as the control and developed a series of 3 treatments to test against it. 1) A quote from Chancellor Mark Bailey, 2) A quote from current President Mark Yarbrough about taking the gospel to the world and 3) A quote from Dr Yarbrough about the credentials and qualifications of DTS staff.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment, we found the first treatment had a 11.6% decrease in conversion with 54% level of confidence and the third treatment showed a 24% directional decrease in conversion with 85% level of confidence. The second treatment showed a 69.7% increase in donor conversion with 99% confidence. We believe this is because the page visitors are more familiar with Dr. Yabrough as they often receive emails from him or see him featured on online courses and podcasts and chapel messages. We also think the quote on the winning version placed a higher emphasis on the need and problem that DTS is addressing through its ministry and mission, which motivated more people to give.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and urgency on a landing page impacted survey submissions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and urgency toward a specific goal for survey takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2022 - 05/12/2022
ACI was running a survey with high volume but a lower than ideal conversion rate. We evaluated the page for opportunities to increase email acquisition. The first treatment we developed included the element of social proof and let people know that over 55,000 other people had already submitted the survey to increase legitimacy of the offer. We visually highlighted this with a live counter at the top of the page. For the second treatment, we added language to let visitors know we had a goal of 100,000 submissions and their survey response would count toward that goal. We also emphasized this with a progress bar at the top of the page and asked for their help in getting closer to reaching the goal.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the experiment for one month, when looking at overall traffic, the second treatment had a 3.4% lift in email acquisition with an 81.9% level of confidence. However, when we assessed mobile traffic specifically, we saw a 6.4% directional increase in email acquisition with 94.1% level of confidence on the first treatment and a valid 9.1% increase in email acquisition with 99.3% level of confidence. Additionally, when we isolated traffic by new visitors we also had a 4.9% directional increase in email acquisition with a 91% level of confidence. We believe this was due to the increase in urgency and motivation by highlighting the progress towards the goal on the landing page for page visitors. Because mobile visitors make up almost half of the traffic to the page and neither treatment was showing a decline for overall traffic, we will move forward with rolling out the second treatment to 100% of traffic for future survey takers. For future testing, we could also increase urgency on the landing page by adding a countdown timer or by indicating a specific deadline in the copy to reach 100K responses.

Where would placing the thermometer showing the dollars raised to that point in the campaign drive the most responses and revenue?

experiments.title: How social proof impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would “joining a community of X people who have already signed this petition” increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 10/17/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers how many people had already signed the petition they were considering signing.
Would “joining a community of X people who have already signed this petition” increase petition signature rate?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a +3.2% increase in petition signature rate.
It appears that showing how many people have already signed the petition drives a slight increase in the overall petition signature rate.
There was some thought that the smaller numbers (early on) for the treatment might cause it to trail the control variant within the experiment. However, upon further review, we observed the following by looking at each quarter of the experiment:
In the first 13 days of the experiment, the treatment led by +4.9% (with a level of confidence of 97.3%).In the next 13 days of the experiment, the treatment trailed by -2.5% (with a level of confidence of 74.3%).In the following 13 days of the experiment, the treatment led by +1.1% (with a level of confidence of 57.2%).In the final 13 days of the experiment, the treatment led by +2.7% (with a level of confidence of 94.5%).
Based upon this information, we saw that in the first 13 days, a valid lift right from the start for this treatment (feasibly, when the numbers for those who signed the petition would be at their lowest). As petition signatures continued to grow, it appears that the petition signature rate did increase, as well (albeit just short of fully validating at the target 95% level of confidence).
Executive Summary: It appears that "joining a growing community" seems to slightly increase the petition signature rate, especially as time goes by, but doesn't necessarily negatively impact a petition when it's first launched (and the petition signature totals are small).

experiments.title: How removing branding and social proof to simplify a petition impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing branding (and the corresponding social proof) impact email signup, donor conversion or revenue rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/20/2020
As a part of optimizing petitions used for the acquisition of new activists and donors, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation looked to experiment with the branding, which also included a social proof element, from the petition signing page. How would this impact email signup rate, and downstream donor and revenue metrics?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 98.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in instant donor conversion rate of 65.7% when the organization’s logo and the corresponding social proof was removed from the top of the petition signature page.
There are two other interesting observations, which are:
The email signup rate slightly increased — showing a +2.8% (LoC: 96.7%), but the decrease in subsequent donor conversion rate also drove a …
Decrease in revenue of -60.6% (LoC: 95.4%).
Could the organization’s branding and social proof (showing the size of the community they would be joining if they made a gift on the subsequent donation page after signing the petition) decrease their likelihood to give a gift at all?
Ultimately, we decided to turn off the experiment because the increase in email signup rates did not jump high enough to offset the big decreases we witnessed in donor conversion and revenue.
Further experimentation may be required, but it certainly appears that when you have social proof to publicize, that it is far more important to publicize it all of the way through the funnel instead of removing it, as it appears to have negatively impacted subsequent giving.

experiments.title: How social proof improved the statement of belief’s email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using social proof increase email acquisition rate of the statement of belief?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2019 - 03/29/2019
After launching the statement of belief on the blog, ADF wanted to experiment with leveraging social proof as a way to improve pledge signature (or email capture / email registration) rates. The goal was to see if people would be more willing to add their name if they knew that they were joining with more than 10K other individuals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 97.3% level of confidence, we were able to validate an increase in email registration rate by 9.6%.
It appears that the social proof element increased pledge signature (email registration) rates when showing how many people had already signed the pledge. This aligns with the social influence theories that when it comes to the psychological or social phenomenon wherein people tend to copy the actions of others in an attempt to undertake behavior in a given situation.
In this situation, when they saw that nearly 20,000 people had already signed the pledge, they felt a level of confidence to go ahead and take that action, as well.
Our recommendation is to continue to test the social proof theory across other areas of the fundraising funnel (on other email capture pages, as well as donation pages) where possible to further validate the theory, or to determine if this is a lift only attainable/isolated to the pledge itself.
