experiments.title: Does adding recency and social proof on a landing page impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the amount of downloads from this month for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it suggests recent ongoing popularity and drives urgency so people will feel like they don’t want to miss out. It also might feel more believable as a smaller number rather than a marketing exaggeration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/05/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on the landing page for the offer, Dueling Populisms, focusing specifically on its effect on email acquisition. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer overall VS the number of downloads this month to determine which messaging could encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment:

1. Social proof language that highlights recent activity can have a positive impact on email acquisition. The treatment version, which featured the number of downloads for the current month, resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisition compared to the control version.

2. The treatment version created a sense of urgency and exclusivity, making the offer more compelling to potential donors.

3. While the results show promise, further testing is recommended to validate the effectiveness of using recent download numbers as social proof.

How to use what was learned in future experimentation:

1. Continue to test different types of social proof language to see what resonates best with potential donors. This could include not just recent activity, but also other forms of social proof such as testimonials or reviews.

2. Consider incorporating elements of urgency and exclusivity in future messaging to increase engagement and drive action.

3. Use this experiment as a foundation for further optimization of landing pages and donation offers, keeping in mind the potential impact of social proof on donor behavior.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor testimonial work on an evergreen donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the goaa/soaa donation page during CYE will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”. because  having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/05/2025
For CYE this year, we wanted to see if adding a right rail donor testimonial on the donation pages would make an impact on transactions. We wanted to test this on all three major donation pages for Focus, the main campaign episodic giving page, the main campaign recurring giving page, and the GOAA/SOAA broadcast pages that generate a ton of traffic each day.
For this test, we wanted to see how adding the testimonal on the the GOAA/SOAA donation pages would impact conversion. We thought this would be neat to see more of an ‘evergreen’ type of donation page even during a high urgency campaign
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the GOAA/SOAA donation page during CYE did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 15.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.8%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different types of donor testimonials or placement of the testimonials on the page to see if there are any variations that could lead to increased conversions. It is also important to take into consideration the timing of the experiment and the specific campaign or context in which it is being conducted to ensure that the results are relevant and applicable.

experiments.title: How including relevant testimonials on the offer landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including participant testimonials for Jan 1 Scripture Memory Sign-up landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because sharing a positive experience from others will build more trust and confidence in the value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2024 - 12/30/2024
Each month The Navigators run a Scripture Memory Challenge via text. Before each campaign, we promote the next months challenge via Facebook ads, housefile emails invites, and text invites. This challenges have been very popular among their audience and their staff has had a lot of positive feedback about them. Therefore, we tested adding participant testimonials to the landing page for our December/January 2025 Challenge.
These testimonials were presented like a carousel that rotated through 7 different quotes every couple of seconds.
Our hypothesis was that adding social proof to the landing page in the form of testimonials would lead to more people signing up for this challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including participant testimonials on the landing page for The Navigators' Jan 1 Scripture Memory Challenge did not result in the expected increase in email acquisition. In fact, the treatment group saw a 4.7% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. While the results were statistically significant at a 92.3% confidence level, they did not meet the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

This indicates that the addition of participant testimonials may not have had the intended effect of building more trust and confidence in the offer. It is important to note that further testing would be necessary to confirm these results and understand the true impact of participant testimonials on email acquisition.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of participant testimonials in different formats or with different messaging. Additionally, considering other factors that may influence email acquisition, such as the landing page design, call-to-action, or audience targeting, would also be valuable. By continuously testing and analyzing different variables, The Navigators can gain deeper insights into what strategies are most effective in driving email acquisition for their Scripture Memory Challenges.

experiments.title: How adding a social proof popup to a membership renewal page affected donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a social proof popup for donors on a membership renewal page will achieve an increase in donation rate because people will be motivated by the fear of missing out.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2024 - 06/04/2024
In an effort to increase donation conversion rate on their membership renewal donation page, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding a popup that showed who had recently made a donation would lead to this increase in donations due to a fear of missing out (FOMO) on what actions others are taking.
They launched an A/B test where the control was the donation page without any popup, and the treatment had a small unintrusive popup in the corner of the screen that read:
“(Donor’s Name) from (State) just renewed their Heritage Foundation membership”
The treatment FOMO popup, which showed a new donor every 15 seconds, also had an American flag icon to align with the same flag icon in the monthly tab of the donation form and appeal to the donor’s patriotism.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This A/B test ran for 13 days. The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The addition of a social proof popup on the membership renewal donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, it resulted in a decrease of 43.5% in transactions for all traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence was 84.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making, but are indicative that further testing is needed to solidify the learning.
Reasons for this decrease may include potential donors preferring total privacy, not wanting their personal information (name and state) shared once they made a donation. Visitors to the treatment could have also been too distracted by the popup's animation to read and fully take in the donation page's copy.
In future, we should test this social proof popup on another donation page related to a content offer or a non-membership direct appeal to test whether they lead to the same results.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a content offer email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including social proof in a content offer email for the housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it reinforces the perceived value of the offer based on what others are already saying about it..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2024 - 02/14/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s resend of the Genesis 1 e-book offer to their housefile, we hypothesized that since this offer has already run previously including a line of social proof in the email to generate interest would impact sign-up rate and donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inclusion of social proof in the content offer email for the housefile led to a significant increase in donor conversions. The statistical level of confidence was at 88.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing is recommended for certainty.

What's notable is that although this experiment only took place at the email level, however the primary metrics impacted were downstream in the funnel. The line of social proof's impact on click rate was negligible, with a 1.2% lift at a 16.1% LOC. However, those who clicked from the social proof treatment were far more likely to sign up and far more likely to give with a 14.3% increase in sign-ups on the landing page and a 59.6% in donor conversions.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential effectiveness of social proof in driving donor conversions. By incorporating this strategy into future email campaigns and conducting further testing, Reasons to Believe can continue to optimize their efforts to attract and convert donors effectively.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a landing page impacts site creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimony for prospective site creators will achieve an increase in site creation conversion rate because social proof reduces anxiety and adds credibility to the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/11/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added social proof in the form of a testimonial on a landing page.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment omitted any kind of testimonial whereas the treatment included a real-life quote from someone who has benefited from created a site. Our hypothesis that adding social proof will reduce any kind of anxiety someone may feel when creating a site with CaringBridge. This quote adds credibility to those who may be unfamiliar with CaringBridge.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a testimonial to a confirmation page may not have the intended effect of reducing anxiety and adding credibility to an organization. The treatment resulted in a 51.0% directional decrease in website creation compared to the control, but the statistical level of confidence was only 86.6%. This means that the results are not considered valid but are approaching validity.
These results are contrary to what was expected. It's probable that the selected testimonial may not be relevant enough to the subject matter of the ebook for this funnel. It's recommended that this is retested in a different ebook funnel to draw further conclusions. Additionally, CaringBridge could anchor the benefits of creating a site by using language like "Join over XXX,XXX families who have benefited from using CaringBridge". This reference point suggests that there are many others who have used CaringBridge successfully, which may add credibility and increase the perceived value of using the platform.

experiments.title: Does Adding Social Proof on a Donation Page Increase the Number of Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof to an instant donation page for people who have signed up for a free video course will result in a higher number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2022 - 11/14/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of donations on an Instant Donation Page by adding a statement of social proof.
We also wanted to see if there was an impact based on where the social proof was located on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test we found that adding social proof above the point of decision (on the donation form) led to a lift 27%, but did not reach validity with only a 52% level of confidence. Even though we were not testing for increases in revenue, we did find that while total donation amounts were similar between the control and variant 2, the average donation amount was around $3 higher for the variant with social proof. Did the social proof increase motivation to give more? Further testing would be needed to say with certainty.

We also discovered that placement did seem to matter...as adding the proof below the form near the submit button resulted in nearly a 50% decrease over the second variant. My hypothesis is that if a donor is to make it that far they do not need the extra motivation to donate, and in fact, could have led to more friction in the giving process.

There will need to be further testing to see if the variables can produce positive results. For our next experiment, we will test the social proof in variant 2 against different copy to see if the way the proof was written will affect the results. And, since we did not have a clear winner, we will start with the same control of no social proof against various statements of social proof above the donation form.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials in a sidebar impacted email acquisitions on a free eBook offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility via book reviews for a free eBook offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 08/05/2022
We wanted to find out if increasing the appeal by adding social proof through credibility and evidentials would improve email acquisition rates on a free eBook offer. We took testimonial quotes from another author and from one of the organization’s trusted pastors and council members and featured them on the landing page to further reinforce to landing page visitors that this was a valuable book offer that was available for free. We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment and ran the test for a month.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed as a 2.2% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence. The results were pretty much flat but overall we saw a slight decrease in the percentage of total emails acquired across all traffic for those who experienced the treatment version of the landing page. Our hypothesis is that visitors could have been unfamiliar with those who were quoted in the testimonials. Because the recommendations were coming from another author and from a pastor rather than a layperson, it could have been assumed that this offer was only suitable for those in positions of ministry or Christian writers—rather than a person in the general workforce or average home. This could have led some people to think the offer was not intended for them and so they did not take the next step. Some changes in visitor segments in the experiment that were interesting to note were that while direct visitors had a 16.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence, email visitors had a 14.0% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. It's possible that email visitors could have been more receptive to the treatment since they are likely to be more familiar with the style and nature of TGC book reviews if they have been receiving regular communications via email from the organization.

experiments.title: The Impact of Aligning a Landing Page’s headline with the CTA and adding Social Proof to the page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by aligning a landing page’s headline with the CTA and adding social proof, we will improve email acquisition conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/22/2022
Focus on the Family uses free video series offers on Facebook to acquire new email addresses and donors. In a prior test, they found that adding social proof language to a landing page improved email signups by 4% with an LOC of 89%. They decided to test this approach on their Praying the Scripture Over Your Children video series offer to see if it would improve conversion. For the treatment, they added “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!” under the image of the offer.
While preparing for the test, they also decided to align the headline of the page with the CTA. So, they changed the headline from Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids to Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids With This Free Video Series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Even though the addition of social proof improved conversion on their other offer, for this video series, it did not. That said, we don't know if the result would have been different had this test not included the additional copy on the headline. Because we changed two elements of the page at the same time, it's impossible to know if both elements decreased conversion or if they worked to cancel each other out.
For future tests we should test one element at a time to ensure a clean result. Also, even in the prior test when the social proof increased conversion, it only had a 4.3% impact on conversion. We will prioritize future tests on Focus offers that are likely to result in more significant impacts.

experiments.title: How adding social proof impacted email acquisition on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2021 - 02/10/2022
Focus on the Family reached a milestone of over 1 million sign-ups for their free video series, and we wanted to test our hypothesis that highlighting this as social proof on the landing page would increase email sign-ups on the landing page. We added a call out underneath the image to “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a directional lift of 4% on the treatment page with an 89.9% level of confidence. Highlighting the number of people who have accessed the free video series seemed to compel landing page visitors to move forward with completing the registration form. This treatment specifically worked well for mobile visitors and email visitors. Based on these promising results, we will look for additional opportunities to test adding social proof elements like this one on future offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.1% decrease in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 5.2% increase in emails acquired with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 10.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 98% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 19.0% increase in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 37.0% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 40.0% increase in emails acquired with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 91.7% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 54.5% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial affects email sign ups on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will utilizing a review on a landing page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2020
Back to the Bible launched a new eBook 7 Secrets of Worry-Free Living and wanted to test whether including a review from the leader of a Christian men’s conference, Brian Doyle, would increase email sign ups on the landing page. All other landing page elements were identical.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we turned off the experiment before reaching a valid level of confidence, we were seeing an overall decline in conversion rate on the treatment. Future experiments will test using other various forms of social proof such as number of eBook downloads or a testimonial from a peer.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials to an online course acquisition page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding 3rd-party evidentials in the form of testimonials increase course conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2018 - 08/03/2018
DTS was running their Genesis online course, and wanted to test the impact of testimonials from other students who had taken the course as 3rd-party evidentials on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added testimonials did not have a statistically significant impact on course conversion rate. It is our hypothesis that the value expressed by the quotes was cancelled out by the increase of friction that came from a longer landing page. To validate this, we will want to test shorter quotes or have them inline with the email acquisition form.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to an email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the addition of a third-party testimonial increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2017 - 08/27/2017
FamilyLife was running a high urgency moneybomb campaign. They wanted to test whether adding a testimonial to an email would increase people’s motivation to give and their belief in the value proposition of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The testimonial increased conversion by 167%. This email only reached 91% validation, but it was clear to readers that the quote from an outside source was genuine and motivated more people to give by supporting the value proposition.

experiments.title: How an exit intent with a thermometer and an exit intent with social proof affects donor conversion rate during Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because for the donor goal thermometer version, the image would highlight what the user could do to fill the gap, and the social proof image would show how the user could make an impact along with the other people that have already made an impact, encouraging the user to help CB meet the match.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
During their 2-day matches, CaringBridge usually has an exit intent that appears on-site profiles, featuring language that reflects the urgency of the match and how the user can help CaringBridge reach its goal.
During this particular match at the end of the calendar year, we wanted to try an A/B/C test with different exit intents that used varying visuals on the exit intent.
The first treatment, which had an exit intent, featured a thermometer image, showing how the user could help fill the gap.
The second treatment, exit intent, featured an image of people, highlighting how they could help reach the goal among the community of people who had already contributed.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices, as well as on-site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment showed an 8.2% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the visuals used in the exit intent may have had a positive impact on user engagement.

There was an increase in revenue for both mobile and desktop, which indicated that the social proof image variant was a clear winner. For desktop, there was a 19.37% increase in revenue. For mobile, there was a 24% increase in revenue.

Control (desktop) - $7,208

Treatment 1(desktop) - $7,515

Treatment 2 (desktop) - $8,770

Control (mobile) - $9,686

Treatment 1 (mobile) - $10,665

Treatment 2 (mobile) - $12,200

So the conclusion for this test is that while both variants produced an almost similar amount of donations, the variant with the social proof image encouraged users to give higher gifts, resulting in an overall increase in revenue.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial to the sustainer donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the campaign donation pages will achieve more transactions because because the donor testimonial will lend to social proof and increase trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/19/2024
This CYE we wanted to see if we could present a donor testimonail on the donation page to increase social proof and trust, in turn increasing transactions.
For this test we added a box right above the giving from that had a donor testimonal in it. We added this to all of the main campaign giving pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It's also important to note that this test only ran for a couple of days due to the stark decrease in conversions. In the future, it would be worth testing this again to see if it just got off to a slow start.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring other forms of social proof or trust-building elements, such as testimonials from beneficiaries, impact stories, or trust seals. It is also important to ensure that experiments run for a longer period of time in order to gather more reliable and valid data for decision making.

experiments.title: How testimonials on the donation page increased donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the main campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they can clearly see the impact of their gift during CYE.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2024 - 12/16/2024
During the high-traffic month of December, we wanted to know if adding an “end user” testimonial would further inspire potential donors to give or give at a greater amount because they were reading about the impact of their gift at the time of decision. Similar to how an e-commerce page might use testimonials for a product, we wanted to know the impact of adding this additional copy from the end user to the Today in the Word CYE donation page would make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the main campaign donation page resulted in a 17.3% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that having end-user testimonials on the page can increase credibility and potentially encourage more people to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials or other forms of social proof on donation pages. By conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform our strategies for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Does adding social proof on a landing page affect the conversion rate on an instant donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that adding social proof language (e.g., “___ people downloaded this ebook – Will you?”) to landing pages would increase donor conversion rates on the instant donation page by reinforcing the value and credibility of the content/Hoover.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on landing pages for content offers, focusing specifically on its effect on donor conversion rates. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer to determine whether this messaging could encourage more donations.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial on the donation page reinforce the need to make a gift?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating. because  the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could get more people to become donors on the giving page by having a donor testimonial on the page itself.
Adding in extra value proposition to a donation page is not a new thing to test out, but with this test specifically we wanted to see if we could present it in a different way where the testimonial stands out and is able to convert more donors because it draws the eye to it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page could potentially lead to an increase in donor conversions. While the treatment did see a 3.0% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 13.2%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95% for validity.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials on donation pages, but with variations in how they are presented or combined with other elements to determine the most effective way to leverage social proof and value proposition. Additionally, it would be important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to generate statistically significant results, which can be used with confidence to inform decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial box with a social proof headline impacts donor conversions during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial box with the headline “Make an Impact on readers like __” to the right side of FamilyLife’s donation page will increase donor conversions by providing social proof and illustrating the tangible impact of donations, thereby fostering trust and emotional connection with potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2024 - 12/31/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing biblically-based resources and support to families. This experiment was conducted during their high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, a critical time for driving donor engagement and conversions.
Historically, FamilyLife’s donation pages have focused on clearly presenting the campaign’s messaging and donation options without additional elements like testimonials. For this test, we wanted to explore whether incorporating social proof could enhance donor trust and urgency.
To test this, we added a testimonial box featuring a real quote from a FamilyLife supporter, paired with a social proof headline: “Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!” This headline aimed to create a sense of community and inspire donors to contribute by showcasing the tangible impact of their support.
The Control was the standard donation page without a testimonial, and the Treatment included the testimonial box prominently placed near the donation form. This was a split A/B test built in VWO with 50/50 split traffic, ensuring an equal number of users experienced both the Control and the Treatment.
The goal of this test was to determine whether the addition of social proof could increase donor conversion rates during this critical fundraising period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Treatment featuring the testimonial box with the headline, "Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!", led to a 6.2% decrease in transactions compared to the Control at a 70% level of confidence (LoC). While the results did not validate due to insufficient data over the testing period, the downward trend suggests that the testimonial box may have negatively impacted conversions.

We hypothesize that the addition of the testimonial box could have distracted users from focusing on the primary goal: completing the donation. While the testimonial and headline aimed to build trust and provide social proof, it may have introduced unnecessary cognitive load or drawn attention away from the donation form itself. Alternatively, the headline or the quote could have lacked relevance or resonance for some users, reducing its effectiveness as a motivator.

Next Steps:

Given the negative trend, we recommend retesting this concept with adjustments:
Rework the Design: Explore a more streamlined and less visually dominant placement for the testimonial box.
 	Refine the Content: Test different quotes or headlines that may better resonate with the audience or align more closely with the campaign’s urgency.
 	Alternative Social Proof Strategies: Consider integrating testimonials in a subtler way, such as below the donation form or as part of an impact statement, to avoid distracting users.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a high-urgency donation page through a slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a slideout for Eagle Lake 2 donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/26/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 during June and July to raise money for their Eagle Lake camps.
We have seen success running social proof-based tests for other clients, so we were anxious to run it for The Navigators.
For this test, the treatment version displayed a pop-out of a statement that read “FirstName from State just donated $GiftAmount”
All the information displayed was data pulled from donors to the campaign. It is also worth noting, that these contacts were pulled manually due to our lack of ability to install a pixel to track conversions on their donation pages.
Our hypothesis was that the pop-out would increase the amount of social proof experienced by the visitor on the page which would then lead to an increase in our donor conversion metrics by increasing a sense of urgency and need to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding social proof in the form of a slideout for the Eagle Lake 2 donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 34.4%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid, and it would be unwise to make any decisions based on them. However, we can assume that the addition of social proof may have a negative consequence in terms of donor acquisition metrics. Therefore, we will need to test this concept again to come to a clear conclusion.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result. Additionally, it might be beneficial to conduct qualitative research to understand why the  slideout did not have the desired effect. This could provide valuable insights that can be used to design more effective experiments in the future.

experiments.title: How including a staff testimonial video within an email impacts donor acquisition metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the staff testimonial video for Eagle Lake 2 email appeals will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion metrics because “people give to people” and the video helps establish a personable connection with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2024
For The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign this year, we wanted to test including a staff testimonial video within an email appeal to see how it impacted donor conversion metrics. The Navigators audience tends to respond more positively to more genuine communication and real stories.
Therefore, for email 7 of this high urgency campaign, we included an applicable video of an Eagle Lake Counselor sharing how God was working within the camp this year. We expected to see an increase in overall donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a staff testimonial video in email appeals for The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion metrics. The treatment group, which received the email with the video, experienced a 44.5% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that the video helped establish a more personable connection with donors, leading to a higher conversion rate.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.0%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
Further, we saw a 37.2% lift in overall revenue at a level of confidence of 78.2%. While this metric is also not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to the inclusion of the video.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of incorporating more person to person connection within appeals, but ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to reach a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to test different types of testimonial videos or other forms of personal and genuine communication to determine the most effective strategy for engaging donors.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson for donation page vistors will achieve more transactions because there is social proof that what ACI does is approved by Trump.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2024 - 05/15/2024
ACI’s Dr. Carson has a relationship with former president Trump. Since ACI’s audience is mainly conservatives, we wanted to try using a quote from Trump about Dr. Carson on a few of ACI’s instant donation pages. We created a sticky bar that had the quote and a photo of Trump.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson on the donation page did not result in an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only 38.9%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide valid and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the audience and their preferences when incorporating endorsements or quotes from public figures. It may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies or elements to understand what resonates best with ACI's conservative audience. A larger sample size and further testing could provide more conclusive insights for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to an instant donation page through a FOMO slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 06/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a free ebook offer through multiple paid advertising channels.
They had previously tested a FOMO slideout on their ‘Renew Membership’ donation page but did not see any valid results in terms of donor conversion. They wanted to test how this concept would perform on an acquisition donation page.
The FOMO slideout pulled the most recent donations from that donation page and slid out every 10 seconds showing a different donor’s first name and state saying they just donated to The Heritage Foundation.
This test was ran for 20 days and traffic was split 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a statistical level of confidence of only 2.3%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to detect any significant differences between the control and treatment groups. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the targeting of the paid advertising channels. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the FOMO slideout on different types of pages or with different messaging to see if it has a different impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How using a testimonial in a Facebook ad affects CTR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a testimonial for facebook users will achieve a higher click-through-rate because it adds credibility to the advertisement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2024 - 05/31/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. The Property Rights content offer has a low click-through rate, and the team wants to improve its performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which included a testimonial for Facebook users, resulted in a 12.7% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic with a 54.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

2. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 470% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the treatment may not be cost-effective compared to the control.

3. The 25-34 age group saw a substantial 1000% decrease in clicks with a 98% level of confidence when exposed to the treatment. This suggests that the addition of a testimonial may not resonate well with this demographic.

Based on these findings, it is clear that the addition of a testimonial for Facebook users did not yield positive results in terms of Clicks for All Traffic, Cost per Click, and engagement from the 25-34 age group.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider refining the messaging of the testimonial, targeting specific audience segments, or testing different types of social proof to see if it positively impacts the performance of the Property Rights content offer. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient and that the statistical confidence levels are high enough to draw valid conclusions for decision-making.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a ebook acquisition page affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for online course enrollees will achieve a higher email sign up rate because the social proof shows the user that there are others who have downloaded the ebook and it offers them a chance to join a group of others with similar interests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client is offering a free online course on the book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page, so we decided to test adding the concept of social proof. We added social proof by adding a floating white box that says “We’ve already had over 3,000 people sign up for this online course! Will you join the group of people who are expanding their knowledge of the Book of Psalms?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding social proof for the online course resulted in a 10.1% increase in email acquisitions with a 75.1% level of confidence. This indicates that social proof has a positive impact on encouraging users to sign up.

2. When analyzing the results based on gender, it was found that female visitors had a significant 681% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the addition of social proof had a strong and positive impact specifically on female visitors.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the impact of social proof on overall email sign-up rates.

experiments.title: How social proof vs. credibility impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of credibility for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will build trust in the value proposition and give the donor more reasons to believe our claims.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/22/2023
In a previous test, we saw that adding a quote from a current volunteer board member produced a lift in donations. We wondered if adding a statement of credibility using the same format would make any difference in conversion. We pulled a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization and put it in the same placement on the page as an element of social proof right before the donation form and split traffic 50/50, sending half of visitors to the control with Ken’s quote about donating and the other half to the treatment with the Forbes magazine call-out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to determine if adding a statement of credibility before the donation form would increase donor conversion. The treatment included a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization, while the control had a quote from a volunteer board member. The experiment ran for 28 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 53.8% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% and thus cannot be considered valid.

From this experiment, we can learn that adding a statement of credibility may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion. It is essential to conduct further experimentation to confirm the validity of the initial results. What we saw was the audience was more motivated by a donor-centric quote from a supporter regarding their experience with giving to the organization than they were by the organizational-centric quote from a review that gave an exclusive reason to support the organization. One reason for this could be the difference in the positioning of the language in the quotes "Let's go get 'em" which insinuates there is work left to be done versus the treatment that claims the organization has the "largest communication presence" which could lead donors to believe that the organization does not need their help or support since they are already doing the most/best with their existing resources and are already in a position to be successful without the donor making a gift to help.

experiments.title: How including student testimonials on a course landing page impacted signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page will increase course sign ups because it establishes more credibility for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2023 - 04/01/2023
In an effort to increase the number of course sign ups on the our Genesis course sign up landing page, we wanted to know how including testimonials of previous students on the course sign up page would impact the number of individuals who enrolled in our Genesis course.
For this experiment, we ran an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment variants. The control version modeled the standard landing page format for all courses, while the treatment version included a text box of two different quotes regarding this course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment aimed to determine the impact of including student testimonials on the Genesis course signup landing page to increase course signups. Results showed that the treatment resulted in a 4.9% decrease in course signups with a statistical level of confidence of 63.7%. Although the control variant won, the results cannot be considered valid since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
Key learnings from this experiment include the importance of setting a higher statistical level of confidence for more valid results, considering the impact on specific audience segments, and the need for further experimentation on the use of testimonials on course signup landing pages. Future experimentation could focus on testing different variations of testimonials, targeting specific audience segments, and testing other elements on the landing page that could impact course signups.
We chose to end this experiment before the 30 day mark due to results consistently being in the negatives.
The key learning from this experiment is that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page may not always be effective in increasing course sign ups, and further testing will be necessary.

experiments.title: How adding social proof affects ebook offer conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof by calling out the amount of people who have downloaded the ebook within the last month will increase conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their critical race theory ebook content offer. We wanted to test whether adding a visual element of social proof—that the ebook was downloaded more than 2,500 times in the last month—on the offer landing page would show that the resource was popular and inspire more people to download the ebook.
We added a grey box under the image of the book with the copy “This e-book was downloaded over 2,500 times last month!”
The experiment was set up as a 50/50 split test where 50% of traffic would see the control and 50% would see the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the social proof treatment decreased conversion rate by 2.2%, but these results did not validate. This experiment is also affected by the change in headline. A more clear learning could be achieved by isolating the two treatment edits, but we can still hypothesize a learning from the result.

Why didn't the treatment produce an increase in conversion rate?
The social proof box of text might have been distracting from the value proposition copy to the left or from the form itself.
 	Social proof might have dissuaded some from wanting to "follow the herd" and join in on what lots of other people have done.
New visitors had a 5.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Internal Links visitors had a 5.5% decrease in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 17.9% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 39.4% decrease in email acquisition with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Housefile Email visitors had a 16.2% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had an 11.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 10.7% decrease in email acquisition with an 88% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 42.6% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 17.2% increase in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and more clarity to a landing page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity to the headline and adding social proof in the form of a testimonial would result in a higher number of email conversions for people that land on this campaign landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 06/28/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof in the form of a testimonial, and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around a 27% for the treatment.

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 28%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof in the form of a testimonial were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and headline clarity affect email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and headline clarity  for readers of a landing page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/10/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. experiments.title: How an alternate form of social proof impacted email acquisition for free trial sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating additional social proof for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition for an offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2021 - 03/15/2021
The long-standing “winner” (in terms of highest conversion rates) sticky bar on the OA Club homepage included a testimonial quote from one user, so we knew that social proof works well for this audience, however we wanted to test if using a different form of the concept of social proof would hold this pattern. We thought that if we could broaden the social proof experiment to apply to more visitors and demonstrate stronger credibility through a third-party then we would see an increase in free trial signups. So we launched a treatment to the sticky bar that said “Join over 140,000 families who have helped their children grow in their faith and learn biblical truths over the past year with a free 14-day trial of Adventures in Odyssey!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we had to end the experiment early to launch another timebound offer on this same page so we did not have enough time to generate the volume of traffic needed to reach validity and saw a 2.8% decrease in conversion rate with just a 21.5% level of confidence. We will want to relaunch the experiment when we have the opportunity to leave it running long enough to get an appropriate amount of samples to analyze. However, over the 10 days the experiment ran, we did observe the following impacts on other visitor segments in the experiment that could help with more specific targeting on future treatments that are developed:
Female visitors had a 113.8% increase in begin free trial signup with a 99% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 98.4% increase in begin free trial signup with an 88% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 79.1% increase in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that the placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that this placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How a new testimonial impacted clicks on a sticky bar

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that demonstrating tangible outcomes in the value proposition for parents considering a free trial will achieve an increase in free trial signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 03/06/2021
When reviewing surveys from people who had completed a 14-day trial, we found a testimonial that indicated the different skills their children built as a result of listening to Adventures in Odyssey episodes. We decided to include this testimonial as a form of social proof in the sticky bar to see if tangible outcomes in life skill areas such as “conflict resolution, compassion and addressing fears” would be motivating for parents considering beginning a free trial for their children. We split traffic between this treatment and the currently running testimonial that was implemented as the control after it had lifted results in a prior experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for a month, but we did not validate this test. While we saw varying results across multiple segments, only a few individual segments did validate. We'll look further into the mobile vs. desktop experience for future tests since it looked like these segments had opposing effects. We can also look into adding variable copy for segments like paid search visitors on any additional treatments.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 41.2% increase in begin free trial signup with an 87% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 20.5% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 97% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 2.7% increase in begin free trial signup with a 92% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.4% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 14.3% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 107.0% increase in begin free trial signup with a 91% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 36.8% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a social proof slider showing who just signed a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing who just signed a petition in a notification window to petition page viewers increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 09/20/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers (who had not yet signed the petition) a treatment that displayed a notification in the bottom/left side of their screen showing the first name & province location of the person that just recently signed the petition.
If others also signed the petition while they stayed on the page, new slide-out notifications with the next signer’s information would display to the person on the page, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for nearly a month and collecting 12,000+ total signatures, we could discern no noticeable difference between the two treatments.

What does this mean? It means that the notification window doesn’t do enough to create momentum to improve petition signature rates.

Our overall team did acknowledge that perhaps showing personal details was a “turn off” for some, as much as it might have pushed more to sign, as privacy is really important to some folks in the Canadian Taxpayer’s audience.

This theory is testable, so further experimentation with address data security and privacy is a follow-up experiment we are looking to run next.

experiments.title: How using social proof on a website can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can social proof help increase donor conversion on the FOTF website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2020 - 08/07/2020
During Focus on the Family’s summer campaign focused around their pro-life initiative, we wanted to see if we could impact donor conversion by using social proof to motivate people to give. To test this, we developed a slide in feature that appeared when a person visited the FOTF website that said, “[INSERT A PERSON’S NAME], from [INSERT CITY], made a donation to help save a child from abortion. You can help too>>”. The feature would show for 20 seconds and then disappear, but then reappear and refresh the name and city when/if someone on the website made a gift. We showed this feature on desktop and mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift (46%) in donor conversion when the social proof feature was present. We also saw the most significant increase occur on desktop devices and returning visitors. The great news is that this feature didn’t have a negative impact, even on mobile. What we can learn is that people can be impacted and motivated by others. Seeing that someone on the site had just made a gift to save a baby from abortion motivated others to do the same. We’d like to continue to test into this principle, run it for a longer period of time, and experiment with the kind of language used to see if we can validate the results and even increase donor conversion even more through this tactic.

experiments.title: How social proof impacted the pledge signature rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing how many other people have signed the pledge increase or decrease the likelihood that someone else will sign the pledge?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2018 - 08/29/2018
We wondered if showing the total number of pledge signatures to-date would improve pledge signature (or rather, email registration) rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only 3% of the required sample size, we did not validate this experiment.
We stopped the experiment to pivot funds and focus upon a new opportunity to acquire names (SCOTUS confirmation efforts), which stopped traffic being sent to this page. Further experimentation is required to definitively answer whether social proof can accelerate registration rates.
It should be noted that the number of registrations (if perceived to be too few) may actually negatively impact the registration rates. Without being deceitful, it may be interesting to also test “large vs. small” social proof statistics and what lifts registration rates the most.

experiments.title: How adding a third party testimonial to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a testimonial from a well-known figure to a landing page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/08/2017 - 12/18/2017
Heartlight Ministries launched an offer called When Your Teen is Struggling, and wanted to test whether including a testimonial from a well-known Christian author, Joe White, would increase conversion on the landing page. The rest of the copy and elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The decrease of 4% on the treatment page was not statistically valid, but it’s possible that the placement of the testimonial distracted people’s eyes from the line of copy, or that the author of the quote was not influential enough to have a strong effect on conversion rate.
This concept would need to be tested again to be a statistically valid learning, and might be more impactful if multiple statements were used.
